name: Kami of the Moon
description: While the exact identity of the Kami of the Moon varies across different interpretations and regions within Japanese mythology, they are generally associated with lunar cycles, night, illumination in darkness, and the passage of time. In some accounts, Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto is considered the prominent moon deity, born from Izanagi's right eye as he purified himself after visiting Yomi, the underworld. However, other traditions may feature different lunar deities or emphasize different aspects of the moon's power. The Kami of the Moon often embodies calmness, reflection, and the cyclical nature of existence, in contrast to the sun's association with energy and activity. They are frequently invoked for blessings related to fertility, safe journeys at night, and understanding of the rhythms of life. Their influence extends to agriculture, as lunar cycles were crucial for determining planting and harvesting times. The moon kami also represents intuition, dreams, and the hidden aspects of reality, offering guidance and insight to those who seek it. Their presence is a reminder of the constant change and renewal that shapes the world, offering solace and a sense of continuity in the face of uncertainty. Reverence for the moon highlights the importance of balance and harmony between light and darkness, activity and rest, and the visible and invisible realms.
subtitle: Guardian of the Night's Gentle Light

keyMyths.title: Tsukuyomi's Birth
keyMyths.description: Tsukuyomi was born from Izanagi's right eye, alongside Amaterasu and Susanoo, after his return from the underworld. This divine birth signifies Tsukuyomi's importance in the cosmic order.

keyMyths.title: Tsukuyomi's Conflict with Uke Mochi
keyMyths.description: Tsukuyomi killed Uke Mochi, the food goddess, due to her unconventional method of producing food. This act angered Amaterasu, leading to the separation of the sun and moon.

keyMyths.title: Lunar Cycles and Agriculture
keyMyths.description: The Kami of the Moon's cycles are intrinsically linked to agricultural practices in Japan. Farmers use the moon's phases to determine the optimal times for planting and harvesting crops.

sources.author: Chamberlain, Basil Hall
